name: Assam
tagline: The Land of the Red River and Blue Hills

places.name: Kaziranga National Park
places.description: Golden grasslands ripple in the wind as rhinos wander freely across floodplains shaped by the Brahmaputra. The landscape feels alive with distant bird calls and sudden wildlife encounters.
places.history: Established in 1905 and declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site, it protects the largest population of one-horned rhinoceroses.

places.name: Kamakhya Temple
places.description: The temple rises above Nilachal Hill where chants and incense fill the air. Pilgrims climb upward to experience deep spirituality and sweeping river views.
places.history: An ancient Shakti Peetha rebuilt by the Koch kings in the 17th century.

places.name: Majuli Island
places.description: Floating gently in the Brahmaputra, Majuli feels like a world untouched by time. Monasteries and greenery create a serene cultural landscape.
places.history: Center of Neo-Vaishnavite culture established by Srimanta Sankardeva.

places.name: Manas National Park
places.description: Dense forests and riverine landscapes create a sanctuary for rare wildlife. The quiet wilderness offers thrilling glimpses of untouched nature.
places.history: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and Project Tiger reserve established in 1928.

places.name: Sivasagar Tank
places.description: Still waters reflect grand temples and Ahom-era structures, creating a serene historical setting in the heart of the town.
places.history: Built in the 18th century by Queen Ambika of the Ahom dynasty.

places.name: Dibru-Saikhowa National Park
places.description: A mosaic of wetlands, grasslands, and forests unfolds across this remote sanctuary. Wild horses roam freely while migratory birds fill the skies.
places.history: Declared a national park in 1999, it was created to protect rare ecosystems of the Brahmaputra floodplains.

places.name: Orang National Park
places.description: Rolling grasslands and marshes create a compact yet vibrant habitat. Rhinos and deer roam against the backdrop of distant hills.
places.history: Established as a wildlife sanctuary in 1985 and later upgraded to a national park.

places.name: Nameri National Park
places.description: Crystal-clear rivers wind through dense forests where adventure meets tranquility. Rafting and birdwatching define the park’s charm.
places.history: Declared a national park in 1998, it forms part of the Eastern Himalayan biodiversity hotspot.

places.name: Talatal Ghar
places.description: A maze of underground tunnels and grand architecture reveals secrets of Ahom military strategy. The structure stands as a silent witness to history.
places.history: Built in the 18th century by Swargadeo Rudra Singha of the Ahom dynasty.

places.name: Umananda Temple
places.description: Set on a river island, the temple rises above the flowing Brahmaputra, surrounded by calm waters and scenic beauty.
places.history: Built in the 17th century by Ahom king Gadadhar Singha.
places.famousFor: World’s smallest inhabited river island temple.

places.name: Deepor Beel
places.description: A vast freshwater lake glistens under open skies as migratory birds gather in thousands. The wetlands create a peaceful escape on the edge of the bustling city.
places.history: Recognized as a Ramsar site, Deepor Beel has long been an important wetland ecosystem supporting biodiversity.

places.name: Navagraha Temple
places.description: Perched atop Chitrachal Hill, the temple overlooks the city with a quiet spiritual aura. Devotees visit to seek blessings of the nine celestial bodies.
places.history: Built during the Ahom period, the temple is associated with ancient astronomical and astrological practices.

places.name: Hayagriva Madhava Temple
places.description: Standing on Monikut Hill, the temple overlooks serene landscapes and carries a unique blend of Hindu and Buddhist influences.
places.history: Originally built in the 6th century and later reconstructed by the Koch kings in the 16th century.
places.famousFor: Sacred pilgrimage site for both Hindus and Buddhists.

places.name: Chandubi Lake
places.description: Surrounded by forested hills, the calm lake reflects changing skies and offers a peaceful retreat away from urban life.
places.history: Formed after a major earthquake in 1897, Chandubi Lake became a natural attraction over time.

places.name: Tezpur
places.description: Known as the cultural capital of Assam, Tezpur blends mythology, scenic beauty, and colonial charm along the Brahmaputra.
places.history: Associated with the legend of Usha and Aniruddha, Tezpur has historical significance from ancient and colonial periods.
places.famousFor: Mythological heritage and scenic river views.

places.name: Pobitora Wildlife Sanctuary
places.description: A compact yet thriving sanctuary where rhinos graze across marshy grasslands. The closeness to wildlife makes every safari feel intense and personal.
places.history: Declared a wildlife sanctuary in 1987, Pobitora is known for its dense population of one-horned rhinoceroses.

places.name: Hoollongapar Gibbon Sanctuary
places.description: Dense evergreen forests echo with the calls of India’s only ape species. The canopy above teems with life in a quiet, immersive jungle setting.
places.history: Established in 1997, it is the only sanctuary in India dedicated to conserving hoolock gibbons.

places.name: Umrangso
places.description: Nestled among rolling hills, Umrangso offers scenic landscapes and quiet surroundings away from crowds. The terrain blends water, forest, and sky in harmony.
places.history: Developed as a hydroelectric hub, the area has grown into a scenic hill destination.
places.famousFor: Kapili hydroelectric project and scenic hills.

places.name: Dibrugarh
places.description: Known as the Tea City of India, Dibrugarh stretches along the Brahmaputra with lush tea gardens and colonial charm.
places.history: Developed during British rule as a major tea trading hub in Northeast India.

places.name: Digboi
places.description: Historic oil town surrounded by forests, where colonial-era structures still stand beside modern industry.
places.history: Home to Asia’s oldest operating oil refinery established in 1901 during British rule.

places.name: Charaideo Maidams
places.description: Rolling mounds rise quietly across a green landscape, marking the royal burial grounds of the Ahom kings. The atmosphere carries a solemn sense of history and legacy.
places.history: Dating back to the 13th century, these maidams served as burial sites for Ahom royalty and reflect Tai-Ahom funerary traditions.

places.name: Panimur Waterfall
places.description: Foaming white water crashes over rocky terrain, creating a dramatic cascade often called the 'Niagara of the Northeast'. The surrounding greenery enhances its raw beauty.
places.history: Formed naturally along the Kopili River, the waterfall has become a popular tourist and picnic spot.

places.name: Bordoibam Bilmukh Bird Sanctuary
places.description: A seasonal wetland sanctuary where migratory birds arrive in large numbers, filling the skies with movement and sound.
places.history: Recognized as an Important Bird Area, it protects migratory and resident bird species.

places.name: Dehing Patkai National Park
places.description: Dense rainforests stretch endlessly, creating one of India’s richest biodiversity zones. Mist and towering trees give it an almost mystical aura.
places.history: Upgraded to a national park in 2021, it was earlier a wildlife sanctuary known for rainforest conservation.

places.name: Sualkuchi
places.description: Known as the silk village of Assam, Sualkuchi hums with the rhythm of handlooms weaving exquisite silk fabrics.
places.history: For centuries, Sualkuchi has been the center of Assam’s silk weaving tradition, especially Muga silk.

places.name: Batadrava Than
places.description: A peaceful Vaishnavite monastery surrounded by open fields and spiritual calm. Devotees gather to experience devotional music and rituals.
places.history: Established by Srimanta Sankardeva in the 15th century as a center of Neo-Vaishnavism.
places.famousFor: Birthplace of Sankardeva and Vaishnavite traditions.

places.name: Padum Pukhuri
places.description: A scenic lake island surrounded by calm waters and greenery, offering boating and peaceful views in the heart of Tezpur.
places.history: Developed as a recreational lake area, it has become a popular urban attraction.

places.name: Srimanta Sankardev Kalakshetra
places.description: A cultural complex showcasing Assam’s art, traditions, and history through museums, performances, and open spaces.
places.history: Established in 1998 to honor the life and teachings of Srimanta Sankardeva.

places.name: Basistha Temple
places.description: Nestled at the foothills, the temple sits beside flowing streams and lush greenery, offering a serene spiritual retreat.
places.history: Believed to be associated with sage Vasistha, the temple has ancient mythological significance.

places.name: Kakochang Waterfalls
places.description: A scenic cascade hidden within lush forests, where water tumbles down rocky cliffs into a serene pool below.
places.history: Naturally formed waterfall near Kaziranga region, gaining popularity for eco-tourism.

places.name: Laokhowa Wildlife Sanctuary
places.description: Grasslands and wetlands stretch across this sanctuary, offering a refuge for birds and wildlife.
places.history: Established in 1972, it is part of the Laokhowa-Burachapori ecosystem.

places.name: Diphu
places.description: A quiet hill town surrounded by greenery and gentle slopes, offering scenic views and cultural diversity.
places.history: Diphu developed as an administrative center of Karbi Anglong district with tribal cultural heritage.

places.name: Rang Ghar
places.description: The Colosseum of the East — a two-storied oval amphitheatre built entirely without bricks or iron, where Ahom kings watched buffalo fights and other royal sports.
places.history: Built by Ahom king Pramatta Singha in 1746, it is one of the oldest surviving amphitheatres in Asia and a masterpiece of Ahom engineering.
places.famousFor: Asia's oldest amphitheatre and Ahom royal sports pavilion.

places.name: Kareng Ghar
places.description: The grand seven-storied royal palace of the Ahom kings, showcasing remarkable architecture with underground tunnels and secret escape routes.
places.history: Constructed during the reign of Swargadeo Rajeswar Singha in the 18th century, it served as the final royal palace of the Ahom dynasty.
places.famousFor: Seven-storied Ahom royal palace with underground passages.

places.name: Haflong
places.description: The only hill station in Assam, often called the 'Switzerland of the East' — a serene town surrounded by pine-clad hills, misty valleys, and the picturesque Haflong Lake.
places.history: Originally a Dimasa Kachari settlement, Haflong became the district headquarters of Dima Hasao and developed as a scenic retreat during British rule.
places.famousFor: Only hill station in Assam, Haflong Lake, and scenic mountain railways.

places.name: Jatinga Village
places.description: A mysterious village where migratory birds descend from the sky on dark foggy nights between September and November, drawn by village lights — a phenomenon that has baffled scientists for decades.
places.history: The mass bird phenomenon was first documented in the 1900s. Scientists attribute it to disorientation caused by fog, monsoon winds, and high altitude.
places.famousFor: Mysterious bird phenomenon and scenic hilltop village.
places.bestTime: September to November (bird season), October to March

places.name: Mayong
places.description: The legendary 'Land of Black Magic' where ancient tantric practices and sorcery have been part of village life for centuries, now preserved through a fascinating museum.
places.history: Mayong has been associated with black magic and witchcraft since ancient times. Archaeological findings include ancient manuscripts and tantric artifacts.
places.famousFor: Land of Black Magic, Mayong Central Museum, and proximity to Pobitora.

places.name: Madan Kamdev
places.description: Often called the 'Khajuraho of Assam', this archaeological site features stunning ruins of ancient temples adorned with intricate erotic stone carvings dating back over a thousand years.
places.history: Dating from the 9th-12th century, the site contains ruins of about 40 stone temples built during the Pala dynasty, showcasing Hindu temple architecture and sculpture.
places.famousFor: Ancient erotic stone carvings and Pala dynasty temple ruins.

places.name: Jorhat Tea Gardens
places.description: The Tea Capital of India — vast emerald-green tea estates stretch to the horizon, with colonial-era bungalows and the world-renowned Tocklai Tea Research Institute.
places.history: Jorhat emerged as the epicenter of India's tea industry in the 19th century under British planters. The Tocklai Research Institute, established in 1911, is the oldest tea research facility in the world.
places.famousFor: Tea Capital of India, Tocklai Tea Research Institute, and heritage tea bungalows.

places.name: Assam State Zoo & Botanical Garden
places.description: A sprawling 175-hectare green oasis in the heart of Guwahati, home to one-horned rhinos, Bengal tigers, and rare species like the golden langur and pygmy hog.
places.history: Established in 1957, it is one of the largest zoological gardens in Northeast India and houses the Assam State Botanical Garden.
places.famousFor: One-horned rhino conservation, golden langur, and botanical garden.

places.name: Agnigarh Hill
places.description: A mythological hilltop offering panoramic views of the Brahmaputra valley, believed to be where King Banasura imprisoned his daughter Usha inside a ring of fire.
places.history: According to Hindu mythology, the demon king Banasura built a fortress of fire (Agni = fire, Garh = fort) atop this hill to protect his daughter Usha from suitors.
places.famousFor: Mythological significance, spiral stairway, and Brahmaputra valley views.

places.name: Hajo
places.description: A unique multi-faith pilgrimage town where Hindu, Muslim, and Buddhist shrines coexist on neighboring hills — a living symbol of communal harmony.
places.history: Hajo has been a sacred pilgrimage center for centuries. Poa Mecca is revered by Muslims as offering one-quarter merit of a Mecca pilgrimage, while Buddhists consider Hayagriva Madhava Temple their sacred site.
places.famousFor: Multi-faith pilgrimage: Poa Mecca, Hayagriva Madhava Temple, and Kedareswara Temple.

places.name: Majuli River Island
places.distance: 347 km from Guwahati (ferry from Nimati Ghat, Jorhat)
places.description: Majuli is the world's largest river island, situated in the mighty Brahmaputra River. Home to Mishing and Deori tribal communities and numerous Vaishnavite monasteries (Satras), it is a vibrant center of Assamese neo-Vaishnavite culture, mask-making, and performing arts.
places.history: Majuli has been the cultural capital of Assamese civilization since the 15th century when Srimanta Sankardev established Satras (Vaishnavite monasteries). Once over 1,200 sq km, erosion has reduced it to about 352 sq km. It was designated a district in 2016.
places.famousFor: World's largest river island, Vaishnavite Satras, Mask-making (Mukha-shilpa), Raas Festival, Pottery
places.bestTime: October to March (avoid monsoon flooding June–August)
